applies_when: User asks whether rooms are still available for a weekend or holiday stay without sharing exact dates, guest count, or preferred room style
User says they want a quick inn stay in an old-town or scenic area but the trip scope is still broad
items.response_goal: Turn broad inn interest into the minimum stay details needed for a useful guesthouse reply.
do: Acknowledge that the user wants a simple answer instead of a long reservation script
Ask only for the next critical facts such as stay dates, guest count, and the room style they care about most
Keep the tone warm and steady instead of sounding like a hard booking funnel
avoid: Guessing availability from a broad question
Dumping every room category before the trip basics are known
Pushing the user to book before the stay scope is clear
items.safe_transition: If you send the stay dates, how many guests are coming, and which room style you have in mind, I can help narrow the next step much more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the trip basics are complete, mark as inn-booking ready.

applies_when: User asks for a same-day room or says they may arrive very late tonight
User wants to know whether the inn can still handle check-in after the usual front-desk or host hours
items.response_goal: Calm arrival-time pressure first and move the conversation toward workable check-in details instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that late arrival can feel stressful, especially with a smaller host-led property
Ask for the property, arrival window, whether the booking already exists, and how many guests are coming
Frame the answer around live room status and host check-in handling instead of certainty
avoid: Guaranteeing that someone will wait no matter how late it gets
Acting as if every inn has a 24-hour staffed desk
Using pressure language such as book now or lose it
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you mean and roughly what time you expect to arrive, I can point you to the safer next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Late-arrival cases with timing details should move quickly toward reservation or host confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether they can park right at the inn or whether luggage can be taken directly to the room
User mentions older guests, strollers, large suitcases, or a larger car without sharing the exact property or access concern
items.response_goal: Protect arrival expectations by clarifying the access details that actually shape the guesthouse experience.
do: Acknowledge that guests want to avoid arrival friction in scenic or old-town areas
Ask for the property, vehicle type if relevant, and whether the main concern is parking, walking distance, stairs, or luggage transfer
Mention that access conditions can vary by lane width, scenic-area rules, and room location
avoid: Guaranteeing doorstep parking or zero walking without property context
Ignoring larger vehicles, late-night arrival, or mobility needs
Treating every inn as equally easy to access
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which property you mean and whether the main concern is parking, stairs, or luggage access, I can guide the next step more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Access-sensitive cases should route toward property-specific arrival confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a child can stay in the room, whether an extra bed is possible, or whether a loft or tatami room is suitable
User asks about two adults plus child occupancy without sharing child age or sleeping setup
items.response_goal: Make family-fit replies useful by collecting the occupancy facts that actually affect room fit and safety.
do: Acknowledge that inn rooms can vary a lot in layout and bedding setup
Ask for the number of adults and children, child ages, and whether the main need is one bed, extra bedding, or a safer low-stair layout
Keep the next questions simple enough to answer in one message
avoid: Assuming every room can add bedding the same way
Ignoring child age or loft and stair safety concerns
Guaranteeing an extra bed before room context is known
items.safe_transition: If you send the child age and the bedding setup you are hoping for, I can help narrow which room direction makes the most sense.
items.handoff_suggestion: Family-fit questions with ages and bedding details should route as room-fit clarification leads.

applies_when: User asks whether the inn is pet friendly
User asks about pet fees, size limits, cleaning rules, or whether a specific room can take a dog or cat
items.response_goal: Keep pet-stay guidance accurate by clarifying the pet and room details that change the answer.
do: Acknowledge that guests want to avoid pet-related arrival friction
Ask for the property, pet type and size, stay dates, and whether the user already has a room type in mind
Mention that pet handling can vary by room, public-space boundaries, and cleaning rules
avoid: Saying pets are always allowed without room context
Ignoring size, count, or cleaning boundaries
Overlooking how pet rules can affect other guests and room assignment
items.safe_transition: If you tell me what pet you are bringing, when you want to stay, and which room you are considering, I can guide the next check more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: Pet-stay questions with concrete details should route to property policy confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a river-view room, balcony room, tub room, or courtyard room can be guaranteed exactly as pictured
User asks whether the inn really looks the same in person as the photos or short videos
items.response_goal: Protect trust by grounding the reply in real room context instead of staged or absolute promises.
do: Acknowledge why the room atmosphere matters to the stay decision
Explain that exact view angle, floor level, lighting, and room placement can vary by room type and live assignment
Ask for the property, stay date, room type, and the one feature they care about most
avoid: Guaranteeing that every room will look exactly like a hero photo
Promising perfect river view, sunset, or balcony angle
Answering with vague yes wording that creates expectation risk
items.safe_transition: If you send the room type you are looking at and the detail you care about most, I can help narrow the next step more realistically.
items.handoff_suggestion: High-expectation room questions should route toward room-type clarification or reservation notes.

applies_when: User asks for a quiet room, a room away from the bar street, or one not facing a busy alley
User says they are a light sleeper and wants a room location to be guaranteed
items.response_goal: Respect the rest concern while making it clear that room placement requests depend on live assignment.
do: Acknowledge why rest quality matters, especially in old-town or scenic areas
Explain that quiet placement, away-from-street requests, and similar preferences can often be noted but still depend on room status at the time
Ask for stay date, room type, and the preference that matters most
avoid: Guaranteeing a perfectly silent or exact-position room
Overpromising on floor, view, or distance from street noise
Downplaying the user's sleep concern
items.safe_transition: If you tell me your stay date and which room preference matters most, I can help point you to the best next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Preference-heavy stays should be tagged for reservation notes or host follow-up.

applies_when: User mentions an anniversary, birthday, proposal, or another small celebration
User asks whether lanterns, cake, flowers, room decor, or a private-feeling setup can definitely be arranged
items.response_goal: Welcome the celebration context while keeping guesthouse arrangements realistic and low-pressure.
do: Acknowledge the emotional importance of the stay
Ask what matters most such as room mood, simple decor, cake, breakfast timing, or privacy, along with stay date and guest count
Frame celebration support as a coordination request rather than an automatic promise
avoid: Guaranteeing free upgrades, surprise setups, or exact decor outcomes
Treating every celebration the same way across every property
Downplaying the emotional importance of the request
items.safe_transition: If you share the stay date and the one or two celebration details that matter most, I can help point you to the best next check.
items.handoff_suggestion: Celebration stays should be tagged for host-note or reservation follow-up.

applies_when: User asks whether breakfast, local tea service, station pickup, or another hosted experience is included
User references an OTA plan, package page, or social post and wants confirmation of what comes with the stay
items.response_goal: Tie inclusion questions back to the actual plan and stay date instead of improvising broad promises.
do: Acknowledge that inclusions matter before booking or arrival
Ask whether the user already booked, which package or channel they are looking at, and the exact benefit they want to confirm
Use clear language that inclusions can vary by room type, season, dates, and booking channel
avoid: Assuming every room includes the same breakfast or hosted extras
Ignoring date, package, or channel differences
Using vague wording like usually included when the answer is plan-dependent
items.safe_transition: If you send the plan or booking channel plus the benefit you want to confirm, I can help guide the next step much more accurately.
items.handoff_suggestion: Inclusion questions with plan context should route as booking-clarification leads.

applies_when: User asks about a full buyout, a birthday gathering, a friend reunion, or several rooms for one group
User asks whether they can use the courtyard, tea table, rooftop, or shared lounge for a gathering
items.response_goal: Move group interest into workable scope while protecting quiet-hour, capacity, and host-boundary expectations.
do: Recognize that this is a serious multi-part stay need rather than casual browsing
Ask for dates, guest count, room count, whether they want full buyout or several rooms, and whether dining or gathering space matters
Use calm boundary language around quiet hours, shared-space use, and host coordination
avoid: Promising a party-friendly setup without scope or house-rule context
Assuming every inn can handle full buyout the same way
Quoting a fixed plan before dates and group size are clear
items.safe_transition: If you share the dates, total headcount, and whether this is several rooms or a full buyout, I can point you to the next step much more clearly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Buyout and group-stay cases should route as high-value reservation leads with boundary review.

applies_when: User asks whether the inn can provide station pickup, scenic-area shuttle help, or luggage transfer
User wants to know whether pickup runs at a specific late-night or early-morning time
items.response_goal: Keep transfer answers credible by grounding them in property, date, and schedule context.
do: Acknowledge the convenience concern
Explain that pickup or transfer support can vary by property, route, arrival hour, and advance notice rules
Ask for the inn, travel date, train or flight timing, and whether pickup, drop-off, or luggage help is needed
avoid: Promising pickup coverage without property context
Guessing cut-off times
Treating transfer arrangements as automatic
items.safe_transition: If you share the inn, your travel date, and the pickup or drop-off time you need, I can narrow the next step much better.
items.handoff_suggestion: Transfer questions with fixed timing should move to property-specific confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether blossoms, snow, river view, terrace weather, or outdoor tea time will definitely look the same as in content
User is worried that rain or seasonal timing may affect the stay atmosphere
items.response_goal: Keep the reply reassuring but realistic when scenery and outdoor experience depend on changing conditions.
do: Acknowledge the disappointment risk if the trip atmosphere changes
Explain that outdoor view experience can depend on season, weather, and same-day conditions
Guide toward the stay dates, property, and the exact scene or experience that matters most
avoid: Guaranteeing blossom timing, snowfall, sunset, or perfect weather
Minimizing seasonal uncertainty
Giving a blanket answer that ignores dates
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the stay dates and which view or outdoor moment matters most, I can help point you to the right next check.
items.handoff_suggestion: Season-sensitive cases should route to property confirmation when dates are near.

applies_when: User asks whether a booking can be canceled, moved, or refunded
User says they booked through an app, a deposit link, or a holiday plan and their schedule changed
items.response_goal: Protect trust by grounding cancellation talk in booking channel, deposit terms, and peak-date rules instead of loose reassurance.
do: Acknowledge that changing a getaway plan can be frustrating
Ask whether the booking already exists, through which channel, and whether the plan is flexible, deposit-based, or non-refundable if they know
Guide the user toward the correct policy-check path
avoid: Promising refunds before checking booking context
Treating direct, OTA, and deposit-link bookings as identical
Sounding dismissive about weather, family, or schedule changes
items.safe_transition: If you send where the booking was made and what the rate or deposit terms say, I can help point you to the right next step instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Refund and change cases should be routed for booking-policy confirmation.

applies_when: User reports a room issue, cleanliness problem, noise, service delay, pet-policy friction, or mismatch between pictures and the real stay
User is visibly upset in public comments or DM about a current or recent inn stay
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first and gather the minimum facts needed for real service recovery.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly and calmly
Move the conversation toward the property, stay date, room or booking details, and the exact issue through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should review the case
avoid: Arguing responsibility in public
Blaming the host, housekeeping, or the guest before facts are checked
Requesting excessive personal information in a public thread
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would be frustrating. Please send the property, stay date, and room or booking details by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to guest service, reservation support, or the property host.

applies_when: User is ready to book, complain, or send screenshots in a public comment thread
The next step needs booking details, policy screenshots, room preferences, or stay information that should be handled privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt or evasive.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with booking, policy, or complaint details
Make the next step specific and low-effort
avoid: Replying only with DM me
Dropping off-platform contact details in public
Using hard-pressure lead capture language
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the property plus your stay or booking details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user is ready to continue privately, notify Catcher and the relevant service path.
